23.1.label: Question 23.1
23.1.question: Describe the factors that affect the capital requirements of individual long-term health insurance business and outline how significant each factor is.

Factors affecting capital requirements: The relationship between the pricing and supervisory reserving bases:

The stronger the supervisory reserving basis is compared with the pricing basis, the greater the requirement for capital, all other things being equal. This will depend on the particular circumstances and the supervisory regulations. The biggest impact is likely to be due to differences in morbidity assumptions, as we may need prudent margins in the reserving basis to allow for the uncertainty in predicting the sickness experience.

The supervisory regulations may require solvency capital to be held in addition to the supervisory reserves. In regimes such as Solvency II, it is the solvency capital and risk margin that takes on the role of ensuring the company is prudently capitalised (as reserves are calculated on a best estimate basis). In a similar way to above, the more onerous the solvency capital requirements, the more capital the company will need to have available.

The required solvency capital could be significant, because the greater unpredictability of sickness rates compared with death rates increases the risk. This ought to lead to higher solvency capital requirements on sickness insurance.
The design of the contract, in particular options and guarantees:
Unit-linked designs could be made more capital efficient than non-linked designs. The effect could be quite significant.

The existence of options or guarantees will increase the requirement for capital (for example, the SCR would increase under Solvency II), the impact of which depends on the extent of the option or guarantee.

These could be significant, because of heavy underwriting. The complexity involved in health insurance contracts may also increase other acquisition costs. For example, it may be necessary to pay higher commission to intermediaries.
Usually this is mostly an issue of single versus regular premium. Individual contracts are usually regular premium but this would be a significant factor if single premium contracts were available.

23.2.label: Question 23.2
23.2.scenario: An established long-term insurance company sells a wide range of health and protection insurance business, through insurance intermediaries, to both the group and individual markets. The company has recently experienced a reduction in the volume of critical illness insurance business sold. It is also concerned about declining profits on this line of business and its current low solvency position (on both a statutory and realistic basis).
23.2.question: N/A

(i).question: Describe the methods that the company is likely to use to monitor the existing business.

Methods for monitoring: The company may use an analysis of surplus. This will help to identify if any items of experience are drifting away from assumptions.
Alternatively, a model office approach could be used.
The company will also wish to carry out investigations of the key individual items of experience.
For CI insurance this will mean claim incidence rates (split between CI and mortality rates if accelerated cover), lapses and expenses.
For each analysis, group and individual business may be treated separately as they normally demonstrate different characteristics.
Claim rates would be investigated by studying experience over a period of, say, 3-5 years, splitting data at least by: gender; age-group; sum insured; and possibly by smoker status and claim type.
The claim rates calculated would be compared with pricing assumptions.
The company may try to identify trends, but even with large volumes this may not be possible, particularly if considering the data for each disease separately.
A split into initial, renewal, termination and investment expenses is also likely.
Any overheads that were not clearly identified as relating to particular products would have to be split by pragmatic approaches, so that CI insurance policies were allocated an appropriate share.
For sensible comparisons over time, adjustment for inflation would be necessary.
Expenses would probably be investigated as part of regular company-wide expense investigations. These could classify non-commission expenses as per premium, per sum insured and per policy.
Lapses on the portfolio would be analysed by duration in force and by distribution channel (if more than one).
Other splits such as gender and age, sum insured, payment frequency and method may be considered if there is sufficient data.
Would like to monitor by individual salesperson or broker to see if any individual sales outlets are a problem.
Care would be necessary in using lapse results for future pricing, because they can be quite volatile, especially in response to economic conditions.
The company may also monitor its underwriting and reinsurance limits, to ensure that these are still at appropriate levels for the developing portfolio.
It should also monitor economic factors and competition, to assess the possible effects of such external influences on our future experience.

Customer value could also be monitored to measure the value of its existing customers. This should take into account the total value to the insurer of the customer, allowing for any cross-selling of other products and customer behaviour during the lifetime of the policy such as taking up any options available.

(ii).question: Comment on the rationale behind this plan.

Rationale behind the proposal: Under traditional CI insurance policies, which have fixed sums insured, the claim amount may: be more than sufficient to compensate for the loss suffered (ie it may be a 'windfall' payment), or not be enough to meet the needs of the customer.

However, the new product attempts to provide a level of cover that is commensurate with the level of financial loss associated with each condition, and so should be more directly aligned to the policyholder's needs.
This reduces the incentive for anti-selection and means that customers are not, for example, paying for cover that they do not need.
The new product may therefore appeal to customers and to brokers, making it easier to sell, and hence hopefully lead to significant sales volumes.
Other reasons why this new product might appeal include:
It might give more comprehensive coverage than a traditional CI policy, by having less onerous requirements to trigger the lower benefit levels.
Multiple claims are possible, and the payment amount may be seen as being fair.

The product may appear to offer better value for money. For example, a tiered product might be cheaper than an 'equivalent' old product (ie one with the same sum insured) due to the tiered product paying out lower benefit amounts for the less serious conditions.
The proposed product offers greater choice for the customer to the extent that they can decide on the proportion of benefits to be paid for the less serious conditions.
The potential for moral hazard (eg exaggeration of symptoms) could also be reduced because, for example, more customers will be entitled to a benefit even if they have less-serious conditions.
Offering benefits for the less-serious cases may encourage more policyholders to seek medical advice for treatments that they otherwise might delay.
This may actually reduce claims costs, if it enables conditions to be treated earlier, and so prevent them developing into more serious ones.

By offering a product that is different from its existing products and (currently) from those of its competitors, the insurance company may be successful in: reversing the trend of declining sales volumes on its CI insurance business; offering the new product at a price that includes a high profit margin (this will be possible since the product will be harder to compare to other CI insurance products in the market), which should help to improve the company's profit levels and solvency position.
The company may consider, in future, extending the proposed concept to its group CI offering.

(iii).question: Discuss the implications to the company of launching this new product.

Implications of launching the new tiered: If the concept of tiered benefits is unusual in the local CI market, significant marketing may be required in order that brokers and potential customers are aware of the products and the benefits that they provide.
New product literature and application forms will also need to be produced.
The company will need to decide which medical conditions fall into what categories.
It may need to obtain data on hospital costs and advice from medical experts, and also undertake customer research, to assist with this.
Clear claim definitions will be essential in establishing what type of claim (ie stage of disease) falls into what category.
These need to be (legally and medically) objective, and understandable to the consumer.
There may be industry-standard definitions which can be used.
These may need to be adapted though in order to ensure that the payout is appropriate given the extent of the illness.
In the absence of an industry standard, or market precedent, it will be difficult to derive these new definitions with confidence.
Claims handlers will need to be trained in order to be able to assess in what category a claim lies.
This may be difficult to ascertain because certain disease types may have a number of possible benefit categories.

However, the financial impact of poor claims control should be less significant (compared with a traditional CI insurance product), if in many cases only partial payments are being made rather than full payments.
IT systems will need to be adapted for the new product - eg they will need to be able to deal with the different level of benefits.

The administration system will also need to deal with partial claim payments. It will need to allow for: the policy remaining in force, but subsequent claims only being payable up to the overall maximum, and so keep a running total of benefits paid; any appropriate reduction in premium following a claim.
Unless the average sum insured increases, average claim amounts will be smaller and the number of claims will increase because there may be multiple claims from the same individual.
This means that claims expenses (as a proportion of premiums) are likely to increase.

There are features of the new product that may act as a deterrent to sales. For example: customers or brokers may see the product as being too complicated - having different levels of benefit for each category and multiple claims may be too confusing for some customers; if the product is unusual, they will not be able to compare it directly with other products in the market; they may not agree with, or accept, the sub-division of claim conditions; and brokers may feel uncomfortable with having the responsibility of deciding on the appropriate level of cover for a client.
The fact that 'windfall' payments are less likely might actually make the policy less appealing, and more difficult to sell (but cover will be more appropriate to customer needs).
Some customers may find it difficult to choose the benefit level for the 'less serious' categories.
Offering a choice of benefit level increases the risk of anti-selection or moral hazard.

For example, a customer that thinks they have a propensity to claim for a condition in a particular category is more likely to choose a high level of cover for that category, but lower levels in others.
The company may therefore decide to place restrictions on the extent to which the customer can choose the proportion of the full sum insured payable, eg you can only choose 20% or 30%.
This will also help to: simplify the product and reduce administration; reduce the likelihood of the partial payment amount proving to be out of line with the customer's requirement on claim.
The company may also need to provide brokers with increased commission levels and training on the new product.
There will be more uncertainty about claim incidence rates for certain categories of claim because, until experience develops, claims data down to this level of detail are likely to be limited.
In fact, experience may change too rapidly in future for data to build up with sufficient credibility.

This increased uncertainty will make pricing, reserving and financial planning more difficult, increase the risk of insolvency (which is already threatened) and increase the company's reserving requirements.
The complex nature of the product will also make pricing more difficult, eg in dealing with overlaps between related illnesses.
Higher margins may be required in the pricing basis, which may make premiums less competitive.
Many of the implications mentioned above (eg staff training) will incur expenses for the company.
The development costs of the new product, and the expected sales volumes (assuming the product does appeal), will further increase the company's initial capital strain.
Given its current solvency position, the company may need to look at ways of financing its additional capital requirement - for example, through reinsurance.
The company could consider making premiums and terms reviewable, in order to reduce its risk and capital requirements.
At the claim stage, there may be a potential for disputes, where customers have expected a full payout of 100% of the sum insured, but they are only entitled to a proportion of this.
The company will need to ensure that the selling process and policy literature are such that customers are absolutely clear which circumstances entitle them to the various claim levels.

The new product may take business away from the company's main CI business, if customers prefer to take the new product. In future, the company may decide to replace its existing CI insurance product range.

In addition, the characteristics of the customers buying this type of product may be different from the existing CI insurance business (eg if it is perceived as cheaper it may appeal to lower socio-economic classes).
This may affect claims and persistency experience, and hence needs to be reflected in the premiums charged.
Different underwriting standards may be required to reduce the effect of selection arising from having a choice of cover.
There may be tax or legal implications for the company.

For example, any death benefit is likely to be more significant compared to the CI benefits on the new product, and this might make it likely that a greater proportion of the business will be treated as life business for tax and regulatory purposes.
Advice may be sought from reinsurers on any of the above matters, and any reinsurance arrangements will need to be reviewed to allow for the impact of the new product.

(iv).question: Discuss the factors the company should consider in deciding whether or not to proceed with this proposal.

New distribution channels: The company needs to assess the criteria upon which to measure the success of expanding into new sales channels.
For example, this could be to sell a certain volume of new critical illness business over the next two years, subject to a minimum overall profit level.
It will need to decide on which specific direct marketing channels to use (eg internet, telephone sales, press advertising or mailshots).
It would be sensible to assess each channel individually because each one has features that are unique (eg the numbers of staff and skills required would differ for each channel).
It may be best to start with just one direct marketing channel in order to focus the company's efforts initially, and await further expansion until experience develops.
On the other hand, having a range of direct-marketing methods may provide efficiencies.
For example, advertising could promote all sales channels, and telephone sales staff, say, could encourage internet sales.
It will be important to consider the appropriateness of the company's existing products for the new distribution channels.
For example, the new tiered-CI product may be considered too complex to be sold through press advertising.
Certain products might need to be simplified, or even an entirely new product range developed, although the cost of doing so must be borne in mind.

The new channels may only be appropriate for some of the company's product range. For example, simple CI products may be sold via the internet or mailshots, but most income protection products may still be better sold through brokers.

The company's group products are likely to continue to be sold through brokers. This is common practice, given the requirement of groups to have someone else to 'shop around' for them in order to find the best product for their specific needs.
The company will need to consider the capital required (eg the amount, source and availability of capital) to support this expansion.
The launch of a new direct marketing channel is likely to involve a significant investment up front for example, in setting up the infrastructure to support a new internet operation.
In addition, most costs will be fixed, ie not related to sales volumes.
For example, advertising costs are likely to be significant because appropriate marketing of the company's products will be crucial to achieving sales to the public.
However, running costs should generally be much lower compared to the existing distribution channel because no commission is involved.
Given the nature of these costs (ie mainly fixed and initial), the level of sales under the new channels will be crucial to their success.
Future sales volumes will need to be estimated, and the market research should be undertaken to assess demand.
The company should consider to what extent it wishes to outsource the operations of the new channel. For example, telephone selling could be undertaken by a third party.
This might be more efficient and reduce the initial capital outlay compared with an 'in-house' operation.
The company will need to identify the profile of customers that the new sales channels will attract, eg the internet might attract young people with sedentary lifestyles.
It should consider the impact that this might have on its claims and persistency experience.
There may be an increased risk of anti-selection and moral hazard because a different type of customer is being targeted (from what the company is used to).
The company will need to identify such risks and allow for them in its product design, policy wording, underwriting, claims control, pricing and financial planning.

The company must allow for the risks associated with the introduction of the new channels (eg increased anti-selection). An increase in risks will require increased reserves and will make insolvency more likely.
The company should consider its need for reinsurance.
For example, it may require assistance with financing capital strain, controlling additional risks and in providing data on its new target markets.
There will be other impacts on future experience of the company, not directly related to the new sales channel, that the company needs to allow for in its feasibility study.

For example: effects on sales volumes, eg the level of competition or industry-wide trends in health insurance sales; trends in claims experience, eg an increase in CI claims due to screening techniques; trends in lapses, eg customers tending to shop around more if competition intensifies; legislation changes, eg those affecting taxation of insurance products or companies, or restricting products.

The company should consider the timing of the launch into any new channels. For example: it may wish to promote this expansion at the same time as the new product launch; reliable systems and infrastructure will need to be ready in time for the launch; it may wish to launch at a time when sales are going to be most likely (eg at a stage in the economic cycle when disposable income is greatest).
The company should look at what its competitors are doing in terms of expanding into direct marketing channels. It should try to assess how successful these have been and if lessons can be learned.

It should consider the impact that this proposal will have on its long-established relationship with brokers. This relationship is likely to be under some strain if brokers feel that the new channels will reduce their potential sales.

It may need to take steps to keep brokers happy so that they continue to sell the company's products, eg offering improved commission terms or providing products exclusively for them. The cost implications should be noted.
The pricing strategy needs to be decided. Consider whether policies under the new channels will be available at different premium rates and, if so, at what differential.
The competitiveness of the products being sold through the proposed channels will be a factor here.
Consider if sales will be sufficient on the strength of the insurance company's brand name and advertising, or whether low competitive premiums will be required to attract business.

Indeed, the company needs to decide upon the brand name it should use for the products to be sold through the new channels. It may decide to differentiate its direct marketing business from products sold through brokers, in order to attract a new market.
The underwriting terms and rating structure for products sold through the new channels will need to be considered.
For example, there might be fewer questions on the application form. This may affect costs and also claims experience, eg anti-selection (as mentioned above).

Financial modelling should be used to assess the effect of introducing each possible distribution channel (including taking no action), against the company's chosen criteria, allowing for all of the above considerations. This should include allowance for capital requirements, which (as discussed above) are likely to be significant.
Sensitivity and scenario testing should be carried out to assess the impact of assumptions varying from the initial estimates.
Looking forward, systems should be designed so that experience can be monitored regularly and action taken (eg to marketing campaigns or pricing) as a result.
These results could be used to calibrate subsequent financial modelling to assess the viability of the chosen distribution channels, or decide whether further expansion is then appropriate.

23.3.label: Question 23.3
23.3.scenario: It has come to the attention of the board of directors of a leading proprietary long-term insurance company that there has been a significant increase in the number of claims that have been declined on its critical illness products in recent years. The board have asked for a report on this matter.
23.3.question: Describe the points that would be made in the report under the following headings: (i) possible reasons for the increase in the numbers of claims being declined (ii) possible reasons for the increase in the numbers of claims being declined (iii) the implications of this for the insurance company (iv) recommended analysis and actions to address the problem.

Possible reasons for the increase: An increase in the number of claims being declined could simply be due to an increase in the number of claims being made during the period being considered.
This could just be as a result of increasing volumes of CI business on the insurer's books, eg in response to successful marketing initiatives.

Alternatively (or additionally) increased numbers of claims could be due to, for example: a maturing book of business - the effect of initial underwriting wears off as policies increase in duration, and so overall claims would increase; an adverse change in mix of business, eg an increase in the average age of policyholders would lead to there being more claims; a general increasing trend in critical illness incidence, eg as a result of medical advances or early diagnosis of diseases.
However, as well as the absolute number of claims, the proportion of claims that are declined should also be considered.
Improved claims control: An increase in the proportion of claims being declined could be as a result of improvements in the claim control process (eg as a result of using more experienced staff), leading to a reduction in the number (or proportion) of invalid claims being paid.
Claims that are not valid: More policyholders may be trying to claim for conditions that they are not entitled to because they do not understand exactly what the policy is covering.

This may be because: the precise cover was not explained properly when the policy was sold; the policyholder was misled into buying a CI policy, thinking it would cover more than it actually does; the policy wording is not clear enough for the policyholder to understand exactly what is covered; the policy conditions might have changed, and so the claim type is no longer valid; the policyholder has simply forgotten what the policy covers, and so will try to claim anyway.
A new disease may have emerged that is not covered by the policy but because the policyholder considers it to be 'critical' they may try to claim.
There may be cases where the policyholder does, in fact, understand what is covered but they will try to claim anyway.

This would be more likely if the insurer has a reputation for paying claims that do not quite meet the claim criteria, or if the policyholder has been encouraged to make such claims (eg by an unscrupulous broker).
Some policyholders may even try to fraudulently claim for a condition that they don't actually have.
An increase in such claims may be more likely if economic conditions have deteriorated recently.
Not eligible for cover / misrepresentati: There may have been an increase in the number of policyholders that think they are entitled to cover, but it turns out that during the claim checking process that they have breached a condition of cover.

The most common reason for this is where it is found that, when they purchased the policy, they did not disclose to the insurer a piece of medical evidence which would have affected the cover or premium charged.

This non-disclosure (or misrepresentation) may be the result of: poor selling practices, ie the seller not explaining to the customer the importance of disclosing all material facts; the insurer not clearly asking for all the relevant information, eg on its application forms (or product literature); the policyholder not wishing to disclose something for fear that it will adversely affect the cover (or premiums), ie deliberate misrepresentation.
There may be other changes that have arisen during the policy term, such as not keeping up to date with premium payments, that has made the cover invalid.
Implications for the insurer: To the extent that the increase in declined claims is due to improvements in claims control, this (at first sight) is actually good news for the company.
It means reduced claims costs, and if there is a reduction in the proportion of invalid claims being paid, it is more likely that cover will be as intended when premiums were calculated.
Also, this will help the insurer to avoid getting a reputation for paying out claims unnecessarily.
However, if a significant number of claims are being declined, many of those claimants will be expecting a claim payout, and so will be disappointed.

Dealing with the complaints from these customers may cause additional administration and associated expenses for the insurer, particularly as they should be dealt with carefully, using well-trained and sympathetic staff.

There is a danger of building up a reputation for selling policies that do not pay out, and so in the long run people will be less willing to take out the insurer's CI products, thinking that the cover is 'worthless'. This will lead to lower sales volumes.
Brokers will also be upset if they have to be involved with disputed claims, and deal with the consequences (eg unhappy customers), and so may be less willing to sell the insurer's products.
Some disputed claims may subsequently be settled in favour of the customers (eg by the financial ombudsman). This will lead to more claims being paid than the insurer originally intended.
If the increase in the number of declined claims is due to worsening claim incidence, then this has implications for the insurer's profitability and solvency.
The increase in declined claims may be an indication that policies are not being sold appropriately.
Hence, there may be higher than expected lapse rates due to policyholders realising that cover may not be suitable for their needs, and there may be further claim disputes in the future.
Recommended analysis and actions: Carry out an analysis of the claims experience and exposure over the period of investigation to assess to what extent the increase in the declined claim figures have simply been due to an increase in the number of claims.

The factors contributing to an increase in claim numbers (eg increasing business volumes or the maturing of the business, as mentioned in part (i) above) should be identified and quantified to assess how they compare with the assumptions that should have been made for them in the company's models - eg for pricing or financial planning.

If the increase in the number of declined claims has been in line with these assumptions, then this does not suggest that the increase is a cause for concern in itself (otherwise, further investigation needs to be made).

However, there needs to be enough expert staff available to deal with declined claims, and so any action that can be taken to reduce the number of any unnecessary declined claims should be considered.
Each declined claim should be investigated to assess the reason for the claim being declined.
This will help to identify where there is the greatest cause for concern, and so where resources should first be targeted.
At a high level (eg where claim conditions were not met or where there was misrepresentation), the reason should be readily identifiable from the claim records.
However, it will be more difficult to assess the reason in more detail (eg where due to poor selling processes), and this may require subjective judgement.
Investigate whether claims control has improved over the period and what impact this is expected to have on the level of declined claims.
For example, a recent sample of declined claims could be assessed as to whether or not they would have still been declined under the previous claims control measures.
It is essential that the insurer maintains a sufficient level of claims control to prevent too many invalid claims being paid, and so discourage fraud and anti-selection.
Ideally, any further analysis over time should strip out any increase in claim numbers occurring, eg work with the proportions of claims that are declined.

The proportion of claims declined could be analysed, split by factors such as: age of policyholder, eg perhaps the way that the policy is worded or the language used is only appropriate for people of a certain age group; duration of policy, eg a high level of claims being declined shortly after the contract is taken out suggests misrepresentation (or possibly fraud); distribution channel and, if possible, down to individual seller, as this can identify if there's a problem with particular sellers; type of claim, as this will help to assess whether any particular claim definitions are causing problems.

A key problem with any detailed analysis will be lack of claim volumes, and so there may be too few declined claims to be able to make any sound conclusions. However, they will give some indication of where attention should initially be made.
If the figures reflect more stringent claims control, the insurer might consider reducing premium rates or relaxing the claim validation criteria, so as to better satisfy policyholders.
The sales process should be reviewed, particularly for those channels or sellers where a potential problem has been identified.
Action could include re-training, regular monitoring and introducing remuneration that is (partly) geared to rewarding low proportions of claims being declined.

These steps may be unpopular with sales staff, and so a good relationship with the sales people will be essential, ensuring that they appreciate the importance of customers understanding exactly what they are covered for.
Consider reviewing application forms, marketing literature and policy wording to see how these can be improved.
This should be done with a view to ensuring that all the necessary information is captured.

Literature and policy documentation should make clear the importance of disclosing all relevant information, clarify that only the listed conditions are covered and that these conditions are clearly defined.
The insurer could ensure that the penalties for misrepresentation are made very clear to the customer when the policy is sold.
It could consider strengthening the initial underwriting (eg by collecting and using more medical evidence) so that more cases of misrepresentation are picked up.
However, this will incur higher costs, and may inconvenience sellers and potential customers.
The policy design could be reviewed - eg remove benefits that are causing a great number of claims to be declined (such as TPD).
Alternatively, the insurer could try to target customers that exhibit characteristics less likely to make invalid claims, eg certain age groups.
It should try to make regular communication with existing customers, reminding them of these issues and could try to educate them as to what are valid claims.
If a new (critical) disease has emerged that is not currently covered on the insurer's policies, it may decide to include this in its new policies (and existing ones if reviewable).
Also, investigate whether competitors are experiencing similar rates of declined claims.
This may help to identify whether there is a problem with the insurer's particular product or processes, or whether this is an industry-wide problem.
If there is an industry-wide problem, then the insurance industry might be able to pool resources to help improve things (eg by improving claim definitions or sharing data).
The insurer could take the step, as some insurers in the UK have, of publishing declined claim figures, in order to highlight to sellers the consequences of not selling appropriate cover.

Depending on the extent to which these recommended measures are successful, the insurer will need to allow for the implications of declined claims in future pricing, reserving, financial planning, etc. This should include the expenses of any actions that it chooses to take.
Moving forward, a system of monitoring declined claims should be put into place, including keeping a record of the reason for the claim and for declining it.
This will enable the impact of these recommended actions to be regularly and easily monitored, and subsequent changes to be made if and when necessary.
The insurer should also monitor the 'knock on' effects of high levels of claims declined, such as poor persistency and new business volumes, and take steps to improve these.
